mappings.rationaleAI: Directly examines the historical and ongoing relationship between science and religion, covering the Galileo affair, Darwin, the design argument, creationism, and whether science and religion address fundamentally different questions (G.2).

Covers the problem of evil (theodicy) from Epicurus through Augustine, Leibniz, and Arendt — directly relevant to the special problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d) and monotheistic theology's engagement with these questions (D.3.c.i).

Covers the philosophical problem of free will including compatibilism, hard determinism, and Kant's transcendental freedom — directly relevant to the special problem of free will in the philosophy of religion (A.1.d).

Directly covers angelology across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam including hierarchies, theological debates about angelic nature, and the fall of Lucifer — a precise match to the doctrine of angelology (D.5).
Directly covers Deism as the Enlightenment religious philosophy rejecting revelation and miracles in favour of reason, from Herbert of Cherbury through Voltaire — a precise match to Deism (D.3.c.ii).

Directly covers the history and philosophy of miracles including biblical traditions, Hume's critique, and theological understandings of divine intervention — a precise match to the doctrine of miracles (D.11).

Covers the concept of the soul across Western thought including Plato's arguments for immortality, Christian resurrection doctrine, and neuroscientific challenges to dualism — directly relevant to the problems of immortality (A.1.d) and mankind's knowledge of the sacred (A.1.c).

Zoroastrianism's cosmic dualism between good and evil (D.3.b), eschatological judgment (D.7), and its role as a comparative case in the study and classification of religions (E) make it directly relevant to multiple doctrinal categories.

Spinoza's pantheistic identification of God with Nature (Deus sive Natura) is a direct instantiation of pantheism (D.3.c.iii), while his critique of organized religion raises questions about atheism/agnosticism (D.3.d).

mappings.episodeTitle: William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience'
mappings.rationaleAI: William James's Varieties of Religious Experience is the landmark study of religious experience analyzing conversion, mysticism, and saintliness (B), and his pragmatist assessment of mystical experience is relevant to mystical theology (D.2).

Covers the purpose, practice, and psychological effects of prayer across religious traditions including contemplative and meditative prayer — directly relevant to religious experience (B) and religious phenomenology (C).

Covers the problem of good and evil in Western philosophy and theology from Augustine through Nietzsche — relevant to the special problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d) and religion and morality (G.4).
Covers the spread of religious doubt from the Enlightenment through Nietzsche's death of God to modern debates — directly relevant to atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d) and religion and science (G.2).
Directly covers the Trinity as a central doctrine about the nature of the divine, including whether the sacred is one or more persons (A.2.b), and Trinitarian monotheism (D.3.c.i).

The Faust myth about a scholar who sells his soul to the devil explores the relationship between human knowledge and the supernatural — relevant to myth and mythology (F.1) in the broader study of belief systems.

Covers the theological and cultural history of the Devil, the development of Satan, and the influence of Zoroastrian dualism — relevant to angelology (D.5 including fallen angels) and religious dualism (D.3.b).
Covers the theological concept of redemption including atonement theories and the relationship between redemption, sin, and grace — directly relevant to doctrines of grace and salvation (D.8).
Explores concepts of space in religious and scientific thought including sacred space in religious architecture — relevant to attributes of the divine (A.1.b) and religion and science (G.2).
The Pelagian Controversy over free will, original sin, and divine grace directly addresses the special problem of free will (A.1.d) and doctrines of grace and salvation (D.8).
Akhenaten's introduction of worship of the Aten as sole deity raises the question of whether this constituted genuine monotheism (D.3.c), and his sun worship relates to nature worship (F.8).
The Fall and original sin directly address doctrines of creation and humanity's fallen state (D.4) and the need for grace and salvation (D.8).
Shinto as an indigenous religious tradition based on kami (spirits/gods) and nature worship is relevant to the study and classification of religions (E) and nature worship (F.8).

Daoism's concept of the Dao as the ultimate principle underlying reality explores whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a), and its distinctive religious tradition is relevant to classification of religions (E).
Prehistoric cave art with theories about shamanism, hunting magic, and social bonding relates to mythological and magical belief systems (F.1, F.2) and shamanism (F.4).
